text.title: Continuous Delivery Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding Continuous Delivery. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale. The {skip-this-methodology} rule routes inapplicable cases.

rule.statement: Every commit on main MUST be releasable: tests green, pipeline green, schema migrations expand-compatible. A commit that requires manual steps before release is a CD violation.

rationale.source: Jez Humble & David Farley, Continuous Delivery (2010)

rule.rationale: If only some commits can be released, the team accumulates coupling between feature work and release readiness. CD requires unconditional releasability per commit.

rule.statement: The deployment pipeline MUST be fully automated end-to-end (build → test → security scan → deploy → smoke). Manual approval gates are allowed only for production promotion of high-risk changes; every other step is automated.
rule.rationale: Manual steps re-introduce inconsistency. Automation is also the only way to make the pipeline auditable and reproducible.

rule.statement: Schema migrations MUST follow the expand-contract pattern: deploy migration adding the new schema (expand) → deploy app code using it → deploy migration removing the old schema (contract). Never drop columns or rename tables in a single deploy.

rationale.source: Pramod Sadalage & Scott Ambler, Refactoring Databases (2006)

rule.rationale: Rolling deploys run the old and new app versions simultaneously. A non-backward-compatible migration breaks the old version and causes outage.

rule.statement: Pull requests SHOULD be ≤200 lines of changed code (excluding generated artefacts). PRs above the threshold are split before review.
rule.rationale: Large PRs slow review, hide regressions, and increase rollback blast radius. Small batches recover faster.

rule.statement: Every feature flag MUST carry a named owner and an expiration date. CI rejects new flags without both fields; a weekly job warns on flags past expiry.

rationale.source: LaunchDarkly flag-hygiene patterns; Spotify feature-toggle audits

rule.rationale: Flags accumulate. After a year a codebase carries 200 stale flags and developers no longer know which paths are live.

rule.statement: Every deploy job MUST have a paired rollback step (`if: failure()` or equivalent) that reverts the deploy to the previous stable artefact within a bounded time (≤10 minutes).

rationale.source: Google SRE rollback patterns; Argo Rollouts docs

rule.rationale: Without an automated rollback, failed deploys at 3 a.m. require manual intervention. The rollback step is the cheapest insurance.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the team is single-developer hobby projects, or when the regulatory regime demands synchronous human approval per release (e.g. medical devices).
rule.rationale: CD requires a pipeline + a team norm. Hobby projects don't justify the build. Regulated domains may require human approval that breaks full automation.
